summary: I was born in Sa La refugee camp in Thailand in 1999. At night, the camp was beautiful with many twinkling stars that made it a pretty place to live. We stayed there until 2002 when a bad storm destroyed our home, forcing us to move to another camp. Sa La was where my early childhood memories began amidst the challenges of refugee life.

Thailand was the country where my family lived in refugee camps for many years. I was born in Sa La refugee camp there in 1999, and later we moved to Mae La Oon refugee camp in 2002 after a storm destroyed our home. Life in Thailand was difficult; my father struggled to find work and faced tough choices. Eventually, in 2011, we left Thailand to start a new life in the United States.

In 2002, after a storm destroyed our home, my family moved to Mae La Oon refugee camp in Thailand. When we arrived, we built a bamboo house, but my dad couldn’t find a job and faced many hardships. Despite these challenges, the camp was where my younger brothers were born in 2003 and 2006, bringing happiness to my family. It was also in Mae La Oon that we applied for relocation to the United States in 2010.

My family applied to be relocated to the United States in 2010 and were accepted, finally leaving in 2011. We were nervous at first because my parents worried we might not be treated well, but after talking with my aunt in Minnesota, they decided to apply. Arriving in the U.S. was a dream come true; we moved into a bigger apartment and I started school where I met many Karen people and others from different races. Over time, I learned English and began building a new life here.

Minnesota is where my aunt lives, and she encouraged my parents to apply for relocation to the United States. When we arrived in 2011, we took a taxi from the airport to our new apartment, which was much bigger than our home in Thailand. At school in Minnesota, I saw many Karen people and other races I had never seen before. It became the place where I started learning English and adjusting to life in America.

Before moving to the United States, my family went to Mae Sot for medical exams including X-rays and shots. During this time, the X-rays showed that my parents had tuberculosis, so they had to stay there for two more weeks for treatment. Mae Sot was an important step in the relocation process, ensuring we were healthy before traveling to America.
